relevant_files.role: Main SecurityScanner implementation with scan_diff and scan_file methods
14 vulnerability patterns (hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, XSS, etc.)
Finding, Severity, Confidence types for security results
Integration tests including negative test for multi-line limitation
Dependencies - uses regex crate, no tree-sitter yet

conventions.commits: Conventional commits with GPG sign and DCO signoff
conventions.testing: Unit tests inline with #[test], integration tests in tests/ directory, criterion for benchmarks
conventions.linting: cargo clippy (warnings as errors), cargo fmt check, cargo deny for advisories
conventions.error_handling: thiserror for libraries, anyhow for applications

patterns: Security fixtures in tests/security_fixtures/ (safe/ and vulnerable/)
Smart detection via needs_security_scan for PR context

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: Primary issue - evaluate tree-sitter to replace regex-based scanning for multi-line detection

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents architectural limitation of current regex approach - source/sink on different lines not detected

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Merged PR that adds test_multi_line_vulnerability_not_detected to document limitation

constraints: Regex-based line-by-line scanning cannot detect multi-line vulnerabilities
Current architecture processes files line by line
tree-sitter not yet integrated - would require new dependency
test_coverage: Comprehensive: Unit tests in scanner.rs (scan_diff, scan_file, line tracking, path filtering), integration tests in security_integration.rs (hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, command injection, weak crypto), negative test added in PR #736 documenting multi-line limitation, benchmark at benches/security_scan.rs

library_findings.relevant_api: Parser, Tree, Node, Language - for building ASTs from source code
library_findings.notes: Core library provides parsing infrastructure, language-agnostic

library_findings.relevant_api: Language::rust() - provides Rust grammar for tree-sitter
library_findings.notes: Grammar crate for parsing Rust code, used with tree-sitter core bindings

library_findings.relevant_api: Regex::new(), find_iter() - current pattern matching approach
library_findings.notes: Currently used in PatternEngine, limited to single-line matching

approaches.name: Hybrid Scanner (Recommended)
approaches.description: Keep existing regex-based scanner as primary, add tree-sitter as secondary layer for multi-line patterns only. Regex handles single-line patterns (API keys, passwords, simple injection), tree-sitter handles complex multi-line patterns (SQL concatenation across lines, complex taint flows).
pros: Preserves existing regex performance for simple patterns
Can start with SQL injection as proof-of-concept
Maintains backward compatibility with existing patterns.json
cons: Need to decide which patterns migrate to tree-sitter queries
Potential for false negatives if patterns not correctly ported

approaches.name: Full Migration to tree-sitter
approaches.description: Replace regex-based PatternEngine entirely with tree-sitter AST queries. Convert all 14 patterns from JSON regex to tree-sitter capture queries. Use tree-sitter queries for semantic pattern matching (function calls, variable assignments, data flow).
pros: Better accuracy - no false positives from comments/strings
More maintainable - patterns as tree-sitter queries rather than regex
Industry best practice for security scanners
cons: Learning curve for tree-sitter query language
Loss of existing regex patterns - need to rewrite all 14
Potential performance overhead for simple patterns
More complex to debug - AST traversal vs regex matching

approaches.name: tree-sitter Spike for SQL Injection Only
approaches.description: As suggested in issue #737 - do a focused spike to evaluate tree-sitter-rust for detecting SQL injection specifically. Create a separate module to test tree-sitter-based SQL injection detection, compare results with current regex approach, then decide on full implementation.
pros: Easy to benchmark vs existing approach
cons: Spike may not translate to other patterns
May delay addressing the root architectural issue

recommendation: Approach 1 (Hybrid Scanner) is recommended because it balances the desire for AST-based detection with the need to minimize risk. It preserves existing regex patterns (which work well for single-line vulnerabilities like hardcoded secrets), adds multi-line detection capability where the current architecture fails, and allows gradual migration of patterns over time. The spike approach (#3) is a good first step to validate tree-sitter integration, but a hybrid architecture provides the most practical path to addressing issue #737's core concern: multi-line vulnerability detection.
